text: automated detection and monitoring of plastic waste in aquatic envir onments using uav imagery and deep learning object detection models marc danielle c. neric mapua university, mdcneric @ mymail. mapua. edu. ph plastic pollution in rivers, coastlines, and open water has become one of the most pressing environmental threats confronting the philippines and the wider global south, yet monitoring remains largely manual, labor - intensive, and spatially limited. this study proposes an automated framework for detecting and monitoring floating and beached plastic waste in aquatic environments by combining low - altitude unmanned aerial vehicle ( uav ) imagery with deep learning object detection models from the you only look once ( yolo ) family. a curated dataset of aerial images of riverine and coastal plastic litter is assembled and enriched through image augmentation and transfer learning to improve generalization across variable water surfaces, lighting, and altitudes. the proposed yolo - based detector is designed to localize and classify plastic debris in real time, and its performance is evaluated using precision, recall, f1 - score, mean average precision ( map ), and intersection over union ( iou ). the expected contribution is a lightweight, transferable, and cost - ef fective monitoring tool that can support clean - up prioritization, pollution mapping, and environmental policy in resource - constrained settings. ccs concepts • computing methodologies • artificial intelligence • computer vision • computer vision problems • object detection additional keywords and phrases : computer vision, uav imagery, yolo, plastic waste detection, marine debris, deep learning, environmental monitoring, aquatic environments 1 introduction plastic waste has emer ged as one of the defining environmental challenges of the twenty - first century, with rivers acting as the principal conduits that transport mismanaged plastic from inland communities into the ocean [ 1 ], [ 5 ]. surface waters carrying plastic and microplastic loads deteriorate in quality, threaten aquatic biodiversity, and ultimately contribute to the

as the principal conduits that transport mismanaged plastic from inland communities into the ocean [ 1 ], [ 5 ]. surface waters carrying plastic and microplastic loads deteriorate in quality, threaten aquatic biodiversity, and ultimately contribute to the accumulation of marine debris that endangers wildlife, fisheries, and public health [ 2 ], [ 5 ]. in the global south, and in the philippines in particular, these problems are compounded by rapid urbanization, informal disposal practices, and limited monitoring capacity, which together allow large volumes of plastic to enter waterways undetected [ 1 ]. establishing accurate, continuous, and scalable monitoring of aquatic plastic is therefore a prerequisite for effective mitigation and policy response. detecting plastic waste in aquatic environments is important because rivers and coastlines function as both sinks and highways for pollution. floating debris such as plastic bags, bottles, packaging, and discarded buoys persistently threatens ecosystem functionality and coastal sustainability, and detecting this debris in a timely manner is vital for guiding clean - up operations and policy actions [ 2 ], [ 8 ]. because plastic pollution is spatially dispersed and temporally variable, monitoring approaches that rely solely on human observation cannot scale to the length of coastlines and river networks that require attention [ 3 ], [ 5 ]. this creates a strong need for automated systems that can quantify where plastic accumulates and in what volumes. traditional monitoring methods, while effective at small scales, are constrained by labor intensity, subjectivity, and poor spatial coverage. watershed macrotrash contamination in particular is difficult to measure and depends on tedious and labor - intensive processes [ 3 ]. citizen - science initiatives have expanded data collection through mobile applications and manual recording, but many of these solutions do not perform automated detection or classification of the litter they capture [ 6 ]. as a result, there is a persistent gap between the volume of imagery that can now be collected and the capacity to convert that imagery into actionable, quantitative

of these solutions do not perform automated detection or classification of the litter they capture [ 6 ]. as a result, there is a persistent gap between the volume of imagery that can now be collected and the capacity to convert that imagery into actionable, quantitative information for decision - makers. advances in drone technology and deep learning offer a promising alternative to manual survey [ 1 ], [ 7 ]. uavs provide large - scale surveillance with high - resolution imagery at flexible altitudes, enabling rapid coverage of rivers, shorelines, and open water that would otherwise be inaccessible [ 1 ], [ 7 ]. in parallel, convolutional object detection architectures have matured to the point where they can localize and classify small objects in cluttered natural scenes in real time. among these, models in the you only look once ( yolo ) family have repeatedly been shown to detect litter successfully from camera images, airborne imagery, and phone images across riverine and coastal settings [ 1 ], [ 4 ]. comparative studies report that yolov5 - based detectors outperform faster r - cnn, mask r - cnn, retinanet, and efficientdet on litter benchmarks such as plastopol and taco, and that lightweight variants such as yolov5s run several times faster while retaining competitive accuracy [ 6 ], [ 11 ]. existing ai approaches nonetheless face substantial challenges when transferred to aquatic uav imagery. plastic objects viewed from altitude are small, low - contrast, and frequently partially submer ged or occluded by glare, foam, and vegetation, which degrades detection accuracy [ 10 ], [ 12 ]. datasets are often collected in a single location or environment, and models trained on one river or beach do not necessarily generalize to another without transfer learning [ 1 ], [ 4 ]. public litter datasets such as taco, the uavvaste aerial dataset, and inland - water benchmarks such as

on one river or beach do not necessarily generalize to another without transfer learning [ 1 ], [ 4 ]. public litter datasets such as taco, the uavvaste aerial dataset, and inland - water benchmarks such as flow are valuable but remain relatively small, class - imbalanced, or dominated by a single super - category, which limits robustness for in - the - wild deployment [ 7 ], [ 8 ], [ 11 ]. 1

these limitations motivate a monitoring pipeline explicitly designed for the aquatic uav setting, in which data augmentation, transfer learning, and an efficient detector are combined to maximize accuracy while remaining deployable on modest hardware. the motivation of this study is to close the gap between the growing availability of aerial imagery and the limited capacity to analyze it automatically in a philippine and broader global - south context. prior work has demonstrated that pre - trained yolov5s provides the best trade - of f between accuracy and computational complexity for river plastic detection [ 1 ], and that newer yolo iterations extend these gains in speed, robustness under variable imaging conditions, and energy efficiency, positioning them for embedded deployment on uavs and unmanned surface vehicles [ 2 ]. building on these findings, this study proposes an automated detection and monitoring framework that pairs uav imagery with a yolo - family object detector, strengthened by augmentation and transfer learning, to deliver reliable plastic detection across diverse aquatic conditions. the proposed ai solution is an end - to - end pipeline that ingests uav ortho - imagery, applies preprocessing and augmentation, and performs real - time detection and classification of plastic waste, optionally coupled with object tracking to count debris as it advects downstream [ 3 ]. the expected contributions of this study are threefold. first, it assembles and annotates an aquatic plastic dataset suitable for training and evaluating uav - based detectors, addressing the scarcity of localized data. second, it delivers a lightweight, transferable yolo - based detector benchmarked with precision, recall, f1 - score, map, and iou, providing a reproducible baseline for riverine and coastal monitoring [ 1 ], [ 6 ]. third, it demonstrates a cost - ef fective monitoring tool that stakeholders can deploy irrespective of location to support clean - up prioritization, pollution mapping, and evidence - based environmental policy [

[ 1 ], [ 6 ]. third, it demonstrates a cost - ef fective monitoring tool that stakeholders can deploy irrespective of location to support clean - up prioritization, pollution mapping, and evidence - based environmental policy [ 1 ], [ 3 ]. research objectives general objective : develop an ai - based system capable of automatically detecting and monitoring plastic waste in aquatic environments using uav imagery and a deep learning object detection model from the yolo family. specific objectives : 1. design, train, and fine - tune a yolo - based deep learning model, enhanced through data augmentation and transfer learning, to detect and classify floating and beached plastic waste in low - altitude uav imagery across varied riverine and coastal conditions. 2. evaluate the performance of the proposed model using precision, recall, f1 - score, mean average precision ( map ), and intersection over union ( iou ), and benchmark it against baseline detectors to assess accuracy and inference speed. 3. assess the transferability of the trained model across different aquatic sites to determine its generalization capability and suitability for real - world, resource - constrained deployment. research questions 1. why is the automated detection of plastic waste in aquatic environments necessary, and how can uav imagery combined with a yolo - based deep learning model address the limitations of manual monitoring in supporting clean - up prioritization and environmental policy? 2. can the proposed yolo - based model accurately detect and classify plastic waste from uav imagery under different environmental conditions, and how does it compare with existing object detection approaches in terms of detection accuracy ( precision, recall, f1 - score, map, iou ), inference speed, and transferability across aquatic sites? 2 review of related literature related studies have explored a range of deep learning techniques for detecting and classifying waste in terrestrial and aquatic settings using imagery from cameras, satellites, and unmanned platforms. this section synthesizes the literature along three themes : deep learning

2 review of related literature related studies have explored a range of deep learning techniques for detecting and classifying waste in terrestrial and aquatic settings using imagery from cameras, satellites, and unmanned platforms. this section synthesizes the literature along three themes : deep learning - based object detection for litter and debris, prior studies that specifically target plastic and debris detection using uav and aquatic imagery, and the datasets, augmentation, and transfer - learning strategies that underpin these systems. 2. 1 deep learning - based object detection deep learning object detection has become the dominant paradigm for litter and debris recognition, with the yolo family emer ging as the most widely adopted approach because of its favorable balance of speed and accuracy [ 6 ], [ 13 ]. a comparative study on the plastopol and taco datasets found that yolov5 - based detectors outperform faster r - cnn, mask r - cnn, retinanet, and efficientdet for litter detection, with yolov5s offering the most promising option for mobile and edge deployment owing to its real - time throughput [ 6 ]. this positions lightweight yolo variants as strong candidates when detection must run on constrained hardware such as onboard uav processors. successive yolo iterations have progressively improved detection of small and visually ambiguous targets. enhancements such as attention mechanisms, lightweight backbones, and bidirectional feature pyramids have been introduced to address the difficulties of small - object detection in uav imagery, where large scale changes and complex scenes cause missed and false detections [ 12 ]. recent work reports that yolov12 integrates attention - enhanced convolutional modules, multi - scale feature 2

fusion, and distribution focal loss to improve detection under occlusion, reflections, and multi - object coexistence, achieving strong results on aerial and underwater litter imagery [ 2 ]. improved yolov8 variants have likewise been proposed to reduce spatial and channel redundancy and to strengthen detection under the poor visibility and light scattering typical of underwater and aquatic scenes [ 10 ], [ 14 ]. deep learning approaches for water bodies have also been consolidated in review form, which highlights that data scarcity, domain shift, and limited generalization remain the central obstacles to reliable macroplastic detection [ 5 ]. collectively, these studies establish that carefully adapted deep detectors can handle the scale variation and clutter inherent in environmental imagery, but they also underscore that small - object and submer ged - object detection remains an open difficulty that must be addressed through architecture design, augmentation, and transfer learning. 2. 2 previous studies on plastic and debris detection using uav and aquatic imagery a growing body of work targets plastic and debris detection specifically in aquatic and uav contexts. a foundational study on riverine plastic examined yolo - family detectors on ortho - imagery acquired by low - altitude uavs and contributed the houay mak hiao dataset, reporting that pre - trained yolov5s provides the best trade - of f between accuracy and computational complexity, reaching a mean average precision of 0. 81 for reliable plastic detection [ 1 ]. in that study, transfer learning improved all models, with pre - trained yolov4 attaining the highest map of 0. 83, directly motivating the present study ’ s emphasis on transfer learning for cross - site generalization [ 1 ]. uav and onboard - camera imagery have been applied to litter detection across rivers, coasts, and low - altitude aerial scenes. an autonomous uav system performed onboard, vision - based trash and litter detection in low - altitude aerial images and contributed the uavvaste dataset

camera imagery have been applied to litter detection across rivers, coasts, and low - altitude aerial scenes. an autonomous uav system performed onboard, vision - based trash and litter detection in low - altitude aerial images and contributed the uavvaste dataset, demonstrating that lightweight detectors can run on flying platforms while confronting the challenge of very small objects [ 7 ]. complementary benchmarks such as flow introduced the first dataset for floating - waste detection in inland waters captured from an unmanned surface vehicle, providing a realistic aquatic testbed that exposes the difficulty of detecting small floating objects against dynamic water backgrounds [ 8 ]. field evaluations of uav - based coastal detectors further caution that high benchmark map does not guarantee field reliability : a comparison of rt - detr and yolov10 on uav coastal - debris imagery found large gaps between benchmark scores and real - world bounding - box label accuracy, driven by class imbalance, glare, and object degradation [ 13 ]. these deployments confirm that unmanned - platform detection is practical, but also reveal sensitivity to altitude, object size, and image quality. complementary studies address counting and tracking rather than detection alone. a plastic water bottle detection model paired yolov8 with an object - tracking library and a post - processing filter to count bottles as they advect down rivers and streams, achieving recall in excess of 0. 947 with very few false positives [ 3 ]. rigorous evaluation on a large open floating - litter dataset further showed that transfer learning and data augmentation substantially affect generalization across sites, establishing these techniques as essential rather than optional for aquatic litter detection [ 4 ]. together, these works establish the feasibility of aquatic plastic monitoring while highlighting glare, submersion, small object size, and site - specific data as recurring obstacles. 2. 3 datasets, data augmentation, and transfer learning the performance of aquatic plastic detectors is fundamentally constrained by the datasets available for training. widely used public bench

small object size, and site - specific data as recurring obstacles. 2. 3 datasets, data augmentation, and transfer learning the performance of aquatic plastic detectors is fundamentally constrained by the datasets available for training. widely used public benchmarks include taco, which provides roughly 1, 500 mobile - acquired images with about 4, 784 instance - segmentation annotations across many categories [ 11 ], and comparative litter studies that consolidate plastopol and taco for evaluation on mobile devices [ 6 ]. uav - specific resources such as uavvaste contribute aerial images focused on small - object litter detection [ 7 ], while inland - water benchmarks such as flow target floating waste from surface vehicles [ 8 ]. a broader waste - detection dataset spanning natural and urban environments further consolidates multiple sources to improve coverage [ 9 ]. these datasets are valuable but are frequently small, class - imbalanced, or geographically skewed, which limits robustness for in - the - wild aquatic deployment [ 5 ], [ 7 ]. to compensate for limited and imbalanced data, researchers rely heavily on image augmentation, dataset balancing, and careful annotation. augmentation techniques including flips, hue, saturation, brightness, blur, and noise expand effective dataset size and improve generalization, and consolidated datasets combine heterogeneous sources to broaden the range of imaging conditions represented [ 9 ]. a rigorous study of floating - litter detection reported that augmentation and transfer learning measurably influence model accuracy and generalization, underscoring augmentation as a low - cost route to robustness when collecting new aquatic imagery is expensive [ 4 ]. transfer learning and hyperparameter optimization are equally central to strong performance. pre - training detectors on large litter corpora and fine - tuning on site - specific aquatic imagery markedly improves accuracy and reduces the data required per site, as demonstrated by

4 ]. transfer learning and hyperparameter optimization are equally central to strong performance. pre - training detectors on large litter corpora and fine - tuning on site - specific aquatic imagery markedly improves accuracy and reduces the data required per site, as demonstrated by cross - site transfer between river datasets where fine - tuning raised map by up to three percentage points [ 1 ]. reviews of deep learning for macroplastic detection similarly identify transfer learning and data augmentation as the standard remedies for limited labeled data [ 5 ]. careful tuning of batch size, learning rate, and input resolution, combined with transfer learning and augmentation, therefore constitutes the standard recipe for adapting yolo detectors to new aquatic monitoring tasks and forms the methodological foundation adopted in this study [ 1 ], [ 6 ]. 3
